title: Crash: Illegal instruction in FastStringifier / JSONObject on Windows (Standalone Executable, Bun v1.4.0)
author: SajanGhimire1
body:
### What version of Bun is running?

Bun v1.4.0 (Standalone Executable bundled with Claude Code)

### What platform is your computer?

Microsoft Windows NT 10.0.19045.0 x64

### What steps can reproduce the bug?

Unfortunately, I do not have a minimal reproducible example yet.

The crash occurred while using Claude Code on Windows with the bundled Bun standalone executable (v1.4.0) during normal development of a large Next.js/TypeScript project.

Observed workflow before the crash:

1. Launch Claude Code on Windows.
2. Open a large Next.js/TypeScript project.
3. Ask Claude Code to perform multiple code-generation and verification tasks (editing files, running checks, and analyzing the project).
4. Continue working for an extended period.
5. Bun unexpectedly crashes.

The application reports:

```
panic(main thread): Illegal instruction
```

followed by:

> Bun has crashed. This indicates a bug in Bun, not your code.

The stack trace points to internal Bun components including:

- `FastStringifier`
- `JSONObject.cpp`
- `FastMalloc`
- `bmalloc`
- `libpas`
- `pas_assertion_failed`

A crash report was automatically submitted through Bun's crash reporter.

At this time I cannot reliably reproduce the crash on demand, but I am happy to test a debug build or provide additional diagnostics if needed.

### What is the expected behavior?

Bun should continue running without crashing while executing normal application workloads.

The runtime should handle the operation safely, or if it encounters an internal error, it should return a recoverable error instead of terminating with:

```
panic(main thread): Illegal instruction
```

The application should remain responsive, and Claude Code should continue functioning without requiring a restart.

### What do you see instead?

Instead of continuing to run normally, Bun crashes and the application terminates.

The application displays:

```
panic(main thread): Illegal instruction
```

It also reports:

> Bun has crashed. This indicates a bug in Bun, not your code.

The generated crash report shows the failure occurs inside Bun's runtime rather than application code. The stack trace includes internal components such as:

- `pas_assertion_failed`
- `FastMalloc`
- `bmalloc`
- `libpas`
- `JSONObject.cpp`
- `FastStringifier`

After the crash, Claude Code stops functioning and must be restarted.

### Additional information

### Environment

- Bun version: v1.4.0 (Standalone Executable bundled with Claude Code)
- Platform: Microsoft Windows NT 10.0.19045.0 x64
- Application: Claude Code

### Additional details

- The crash report was automatically submitted through Bun's built-in crash reporter.
- I have attached the complete crash stack trace.
- At the moment, I do not have a minimal reproducible example.
- If there are debug builds or additional logging options that would help identify the cause, I'd be happy to test them and provide further information.

title: Writable.toWeb() breaks when a preload script requires node:stream
author: cirospaciari
body:
### What version of Bun is running?

1.3.14 (and current `main`)

### What steps can reproduce the bug?

`Writable.toWeb()` returns a broken WritableStream when *any* preload script has required `node:stream` (or `node:zlib`).

`preload.js`:
```js
require("node:stream");
```

`repro.js`:
```js
const { Writable } = require("stream");
const w = new Writable({ write(c, e, cb) { cb(); } });
const ws = Writable.toWeb(w);
ws.close().then(
  () => console.log("RESOLVED (wrong)"),
  e => console.log("rejected:", e.code, "(correct)"),
);
w.end();
```

```
bun repro.js                      # rejected: ABORT_ERR (correct)
bun --preload ./preload.js repro.js   # RESOLVED (wrong) + TypeError below
node --require ./preload.js repro.js  # rejected: ABORT_ERR (correct)
```

### What is the expected behavior?

Same as without the preload, and same as Node: ending the underlying `stream.Writable` errors the WritableStream, so `ws.close()` rejects with `ABORT_ERR`.

### What do you see instead?

`ws.close()` resolves instead of rejecting, and the end-of-stream callback then throws:

```
TypeError: undefined is not an object (evaluating 'handleKnownInternalErrors')
      at <anonymous> (internal:webstreams_adapters:204:15)
      at <anonymous> (internal:shared:113:35)
      at onclose (internal:streams/end-of-stream:170:21)
      at emit (node:events:142:22)
      at emitCloseNT (internal:streams/destroy:104:14)
```

`handleKnownInternalErrors` is a plain function declaration in the same builtin module
(`src/js/internal/webstreams_adapters.ts`) as its two call sites, so it should never be
undefined. It only becomes undefined when the module is first pulled in from a preload
script, which suggests the builtin's module-scope bindings are not fully initialized on that
path — `newWritableStreamFromStreamWritable`'s `eos()` callback then faults, and the
`closed` promise settles the wrong way.

### Additional information

Reproduces on released 1.3.14 and on current `main` (i.e. it predates the C++ web-streams
rewrite in #33193). `node:zlib` in the preload triggers it too — `webstreams_adapters.ts`
reads `processBindingConstants.zlib` at module scope.

Found while porting Node's upstream web-streams tests (#32627): it makes
`test/parallel/test-whatwg-webstreams-adapters-to-writablestream.js` fail under the
node-test harness, whose preload requires `node:stream`. That file is left out of the
vendored set until this is fixed.

title: ASAN CI: JSC assertion in JSObject::getOwnPropertyDescriptor during worker terminate (test-worker-message-port-transfer-terminate)
author: cirospaciari
body:
On the debian-13 x64-asan test lane, `test/js/node/test/parallel/test-worker-message-port-transfer-terminate.js` intermittently dies with:

```
ASSERTION FAILED: !scope.exception() || !result
vendor/WebKit/Source/JavaScriptCore/runtime/JSObject.cpp(3936) : bool JSC::JSObject::getOwnPropertyDescriptor(JSGlobalObject *, PropertyName, PropertyDescriptor &)
main process killed by SIGABRT
```

- Occurs on main as well as feature branches (CI's annotation tagger marks it `[pre-existing]`; observed on builds 72522 and 72563 of PR #32488, which does not touch worker/JSC property machinery).
- Mechanism: a property access during worker terminate/transfer returns a result while an exception is pending — an exception-check invariant violation in the bindings that call `getOwnPropertyDescriptor` on the transfer/terminate path.
- Suggested approach: run the worker message-port transfer + terminate suites under `BUN_JSC_validateExceptionChecks=1` on an ASAN/debug build and audit the property-descriptor call sites the trap identifies (likely a missing `RETURN_IF_EXCEPTION` after a user-code-reachable get).

Filing so the failure has a tracker independent of unrelated PRs whose CI it appears on.

title: Bun.build renames a function parameter to shadow a referenced global function
author: theopak
body:
### What version of Bun is running?

1.3.14

### What platform is your computer?

macOS 26.5.1 (25F80), arm64

### What steps can reproduce the bug?

This file reproduces the bug when bundled with the steps below: https://gist.github.com/theopak/6536127d85204bfc4b1dc7a8dc6890a6#file-bun-symbol-resolution-repro-js

First, run the input directly as a control:

```sh
bun bun-symbol-resolution-repro.js
```

Then bundle and run it:

```sh
bun build bun-symbol-resolution-repro.js --outdir out
bun out/bun-symbol-resolution-repro.js
```

The failure reproduced in three consecutive clean runs.

### What is the expected behavior?

Bundling valid JavaScript should preserve its symbol bindings.

The input contains the equivalent of:

```js
async function J7A(A, B, Q) {
  const result = A3(B.projectId, null);
}
```

`A3` is a global function. The parameter `A` and global `A3` are different symbols.

### What do you see instead?

Bun renames the parameter `A` to `A3`, shadowing the referenced global function:

```js
async function J7A(A3, B, Q) {
  const result = A3(B.projectId, null);
}
```

The bundled output then fails:

```text
TypeError: A3 is not a function.
(In 'A3(g, null)', 'A3' is an instance of Object)
    at J7A
```

Directly running the input does not produce this collision.

This is a severe correctness failure because a successful production build can silently change valid program semantics and ship type-confused runtime crashes in code that worked before bundling.

### Additional information

This requires only one entrypoint and a normal `bun build`. Compilation and bytecode generation are not involved.

The fixture is intentionally large because the failure depends on the scope graph and identifier-renaming order. Removing unrelated scopes can change the assigned names and make the failure disappear. The core problem is that the identifier renamer assigns a function parameter the same name as a referenced global, changing program semantics.

_AI Disclosure: Repro case and issue text created by GPT-5.6-Sol and reviewed by me._

title: BUG: Importing chalk makes process.stdin async iteration empty for redirected files
author: vshat
body:
### What version of Bun is running?

1.3.14+0d9b296af

### What platform is your computer?

Darwin 24.6.0 arm64 arm

### What steps can reproduce the bug?

```shell
bun add chalk
# bun add v1.3.14 (0d9b296a)

# installed chalk@5.6.2


printf 'hello\n' > input.txt

bun -e 'for await (const x of process.stdin) process.stdout.write(x)' \
  < input.txt
# Expected / actual: prints "hello"

bun -e 'import "chalk"; for await (const x of process.stdin) process.stdout.write(x)' \
  < input.txt
# Expected: prints "hello"
# Actual: prints nothing

bun -e 'import "chalk"; let n = 0; for await (const x of process.stdin) n += x.length; console.error(n)' \
  < input.txt
# Expected: 6
# Actual: 0

bun -e 'import "chalk"; const x = await Bun.stdin.text(); console.error(x.length)' \
  < input.txt
# Expected / actual: 6
```

### What is the expected behavior?

`process.stdin`, the Node-compatible readable stream, should remain readable after importing `Chalk` and should return the contents of stdin redirected from a regular file.

### What do you see instead?

After `import "chalk"`, async iteration of `process.stdin` immediately ends without chunks. `Bun.stdin.text()` can still read the same `stdin` content.

### Additional information

_No response_

title: Bug: spawnSync never returns: child exit lost (child stays zombie), wait loop busy-spins at 100% CPU re-registering a finished pipe reader (macOS ARM64)
author: iOSonntag
body:
### What version of Bun is running?

1.3.13+bf2e2cecf (behavior identical on 1.3.14; all stack traces below were captured with the published
`bun-darwin-aarch64-profile` build of 1.3.13, which shares the release binary's Mach-O UUID
`4A17CD88-9E15-3FED-B870-A9FE3ED3C3C8`, so symbols and line numbers are exact)

### What platform is your computer?

Darwin 25.2.0 arm64 arm (macOS 26.2 build 25C56, Apple Silicon, 12 cores)

### What steps can reproduce the bug?

We can reproduce it reliably, but only in a real-world-sized workload so far:

- `bun test --parallel` over a suite of 326 files / ~2,100 tests in which many tests exercise code that calls
  `node:child_process.execFileSync("git", [...])` (bun routes this through `Bun.spawnSync` →
  `spawnMaybeSync`). The same worker processes also run in-process HTTP servers (`Bun.serve` + `fetch`), SSE
  streams, timers, async `Bun.spawn`s, and `bun:sqlite` — i.e. lots of fd churn around the sync spawns.
- On an idle machine, **~1 in 5 full-suite runs never finishes**: suite output stops, the coordinator goes
  idle, and one or more `--test-worker` processes peg a core at ~100 % CPU forever. Two workers wedged
  simultaneously in one observed run.
- **Worker parallelism is not required**: the same suite run serially (`bun test --isolate`, no `--parallel`,
  single process, files one-at-a-time) wedged the same way with the same stack. The probability appears to
  scale with the number of `spawnSync` calls, not with concurrency.
- Under external CPU load the incidence drops to ~zero (timing race: slower children widen whatever window is
  being missed).
- What we could NOT do: distill it to a standalone snippet. A synthetic 12-file suite doing 2,400 bare
  `execFileSync("git", ["--version"])` calls per run produced **zero wedges in 40 runs (~96 k spawns)**. The
  trigger seems to need the surrounding event-loop/fd activity, which is why we suspect stale poll or
  fd-recycling state on the per-VM singleton `SpawnSyncEventLoop` kqueue. We're happy to run instrumented
  builds or additional diagnostics against the real workload.

### What is the expected behavior?

`spawnSync` returns when the child exits. Worst case — if the runtime somehow misses the exit — the
`bun:test` per-test `--timeout` fails the test and the suite continues.

### What do you see instead?

At wedge time the worker always has exactly one child, and it is a **zombie** — the child exited, bun never
collected it:

```
  PID  PPID STAT %CPU ELAPSED COMMAND
27157 27125 R    99.9   00:57 bun test --test-worker --isolate --timeout=120000 --max-concurrency=20 ...
29677 27157 Z     0.0   00:44 <defunct>          <- the spawnSync'd git: exited 40+ s ago, unreaped
```

`lsof -p 27157` shows **zero pipe fds** — the child's stdout/stderr pipes were already drained and closed.

`sample 27157 3` main thread (2,455 samples; a second sample 5 s later is identical, i.e. no forward
progress; line numbers are from the 1.3.13 profile dSYM):

```
BunObject_callback_spawnSync (host_fn.zig:19)
  bun.js.api.bun.js_bun_spawn_bindings.spawnMaybeSync (js_bun_spawn_bindings.zig ~1014)
    86%  io.PipeReader.PosixBufferedReader.registerPoll (PipeReader.zig:291)
           posix_event_loop.FilePoll.registerWithFd (posix_event_loop.zig:900)
             kevent64                               <- a fresh registration syscall EVERY loop iteration
     8%  spawnMaybeSync (js_bun_spawn_bindings.zig:958/984/990)
           bun.js.event_loop.SpawnSyncEventLoop.tickWithTimeout (SpawnSyncEventLoop.zig:144/168/177)
                                                    <- only clock_gettime / pthread_rwlock frames:
                                                       returns immediately, never blocks in kevent
```

Reading this against the `bun-v1.3.13` source: the child's exit notification was lost, so
`subprocess.computeHasPendingActivity()` never flips; the sync wait loop's unconditional per-iteration
`subprocess.stdout.pipe.watch()` / `stderr.pipe.watch()` keeps re-arming readers that already finished; and
`tickWithTimeout` returns immediately in this state. Net result: an infinite hot spin that emits nothing —
and `bun test`'s own per-test timeout cannot fire normally because the event loop never leaves the native
wait.

**Escalation via the bun:test timeout.** If the wedged worker survives past the test's `--timeout`
(120 s here), the timeout fires *inside* the stuck pump and synchronously advances the runner — starting the
NEXT test on top of the dead spawnSync's stack. When that test also shells out, a second sync wait nests. An
orphaned worker sampled 3.4 hours into this state shows the whole chain in one stack:

```
event_loop.autoTick -> Timer.All.drainTimers -> TimerObjectInternals.fire -> drainMicrotasks
  -> [JS] -> BunObject_callback_spawnSync                       <- spawnSync #1 (the stuck one)
    -> spawnMaybeSync (js_bun_spawn_bindings.zig:1014)
      -> BunTest.bunTestTimeoutCallback (bun_test.zig:504)      <- per-test timeout fires inside the pump
        -> BunTest.run -> Execution.stepGroup (Execution.zig:290) -> stepSequence (:348)
          -> runTestCallback -> [JS of the NEXT test]
            -> BunObject_callback_spawnSync                     <- spawnSync #2, nested, spinning forever
              -> spawnMaybeSync -> registerPoll -> kevent64
```

The comment in `spawnMaybeSync` ("There is a scenario where inside of spawnSync() a totally different test
fails, and that SHOULD be okay") suggests the reentry is intentional, but once the outer spawnSync can never
complete, the runner state is unrecoverable and the process spins at 100 % CPU until killed.

### Additional information

Suggested hardening — any one of these would unstick it:

1. In `spawnMaybeSync`'s wait loop, when a tick makes no progress N times (or on a periodic slow path), call
   `waitpid(pid, WNOHANG)` directly — this both reaps the zombie and recovers from the lost exit event.
2. Skip `.watch()` for pipe readers that are already done/closed — turns a lost exit into a cheap blocked
   wait (which the bun:test timeout can still shoot) instead of a 100 %-CPU spin.
3. Don't advance the test-runner state machine (`runTestCallback`) from inside an active spawnSync pump —
   mark the test failed and unwind instead.

Possibly related (same zero-timeout-kevent-spin symptom family, different triggers): #27766 (requires Ink +
streaming fetch), #26811, #25652.

*(Note: this was debugged and written by Fable max - all other models and efforts I tested failed to nail the problem)*

title: SIGTRAP: C++ exception in __cxa_finalize_ranges at exit after in-process onnxruntime (via @huggingface/transformers) inference — tests pass, teardown panics
author: ApotheMonk
body:
### What version of Bun is running?

1.3.10 (30e609e0)

### What platform is your computer?

macOS 26.5.1 arm64 (Apple Silicon)

### What steps can reproduce the bug?

Run real ONNX inference in-process via `@huggingface/transformers` (which dlopens `libonnxruntime.1.21.0.dylib` through onnxruntime-node), let the tests/script complete, and let the process exit normally.

```ts
// bun test repro shape — any test that does real in-process ONNX inference:
import { pipeline } from "@huggingface/transformers";
const embed = await pipeline("feature-extraction", "jinaai/jina-embeddings-v3");
await embed("hello world");
// ... all assertions pass ...
// crash fires AFTER results are printed, during process exit
```

Observed consistently (3/3 standalone runs, 40+ identical crash reports over 5 days): the test file prints `11 pass / 0 fail ... Ran 11 tests`, then `panic(main thread): A C++ exception occurred / oh no: Bun has crashed` with SIGTRAP. Any `bun test` set that includes the ONNX-exercising file crashes; the same set without it exits 0. Bisection ruled out co-resident native modules (sqlite-vec `vec0.dylib`, sharp `libvips-cpp`, homebrew libsqlite3) — each is clean alone and in pairs; only the onnxruntime-exercising file triggers it.

### What is the expected behavior?

Process exits 0 after tests pass.

### What happens instead?

SIGTRAP (`EXC_BREAKPOINT`) on the main thread inside C++ static-destructor finalization at exit:

```
libc++abi.dylib   std::__terminate(void (*)())
libc++abi.dylib   std::terminate()
libsystem_c.dylib __cxa_finalize_ranges
libsystem_c.dylib exit
bun               (atexit/main teardown)
```

ONNX Runtime registers C++ global/static destructors + background thread pools; after real inference has run, one of those destructors throws in a `noexcept` teardown context during `__cxa_finalize_ranges` → `std::terminate()` → SIGTRAP. Bun's own crash line notes `napi_module_register process_dlopen(2) exited` — a dlopen'd napi module, panic at exit.

bun.report (redacted stack from the live repro):
https://bun.report/1.3.10/Mt130e609egjmQgwggCm3629C2stx+Cm6v6sB____2prB2qhsCmil9Juiz1kBu9m+EmsoqF2hrB+giB

### Additional information

- The crash is purely at teardown — all test results are final and correct before it fires. Exit code is the only casualty (breaks CI/test-gating on exit codes).
- Workaround we ship: shard ONNX-exercising tests and judge them on the printed summary instead of exit code; and route inference out-of-process. A `_exit(0)`-style skip of static dtors also avoids it, but there's no supported hook in `bun test`.
- Possibly related family: dlopen'd napi module destructors running at exit generally; #30286 was a different NAPI teardown crash (Worker exitAndDeinit) and is closed.

title: Segfault at 0x3C — nProtect GameGuard (npggNT64.des) injection kills any Bun process on Windows 11
author: aron-thor-haraldsson
body:
### What version of Bun is running? 1.3.14+0d9b296af ### What platform is your computer? Microsoft Windows NT 10.0.26100.0 x64 ### What steps can reproduce the bug? Any Bun process on Windows segfaults at address 0x3C while nProtect GameGuard is running. A process that is already running and idle is killed within seconds of GameGuard starting — it does not need to be doing anything. Repro: Install a game that uses nProtect GameGuard. Confirmed with Helldivers 2 and Myth of Empires: Throne (both on Steam). With no game running, start a long-lived Bun process: ```powershell bun -e "let n=0; setInterval(()=>{ for(let i=0;i<5e6;i++) n+=i; console.log('alive', Date.now()); }, 1000)" ``` It ticks once per second indefinitely. No crash. Leave it running. Launch the game — this auto-starts the GameGuard service, which injects npggNT64.des into processes across the desktop. Within seconds, the already-running, previously healthy Bun process dies with a segfault at 0x3C. Quit the game. GameGuard exits. Bun works normally again immediately — no reboot, no reinstall. Steps 3–5 cycle cleanly and repeatedly (verified 4+ times). This also reproduces on Bun 1.4.0 in a bun build --compile standalone executable (details in Additional information), so it is not specific to one version or to compiled binaries. ### What is the expected behavior? The Bun process keeps running and continues logging alive once per second, regardless of whether an unrelated third-party process (a game and its anti-cheat service) is running elsewhere on the system.`` ### What do you see instead? The running Bun process dies. Crash output: > ============================================================ > Bun v1.3.14 (0d9b296a) Windows x64 > Windows v.win11_dt > CPU: sse42 avx avx2 > Args: "C:\Users\\.bun\bin\bun.exe" "-e" "let n=0; setInterval(()=>{ for(let i=0;i<5e6;i++) n+=i; console.log('alive', Date.now()); }, 1000)" > Features: jsc > Builtins: "bun:main" > Elapsed: 482ms | User: 0ms | Sys: 93ms > RSS: 52.58MB | Peak: 52.58MB | Commit: 0.24GB | Faults: 13114 | Machine: 33.72GB > panic(thread 21900): Segmentation fault at address 0x3C > oh no: Bun has crashed. This indicates a bug in Bun, not your code. > To send a redacted crash report to Bun's team, > please file a GitHub issue using the link below: > https://bun.report/1.3.14/wa10d9b296AggggCCYnpggNT64.desy2TCYnpggNT64.desynouE__CYnpggNT64.desws0PCUUSER32.dllglkwBCYnpggNT64.desgi6R____CYnpggNT64.desoqmWA2A4D Every subsequent bun invocation also crashes the same way for as long as GameGuard is running. The frames in the crash report are all in GameGuard's injected DLL, with a USER32.dll frame between them: npggNT64.des … npggNT64.des … USER32.dll … npggNT64.des … npggNT64.des ### Additional information

The faulting code is in an injected third-party DLL

npggNT64.des is nProtect GameGuard's usermode hook/injection module. Bun reports "a bug in Bun" because it catches the signal, but the faulting instruction is in foreign code executing inside Bun's address space.

The USER32.dll frame sandwiched between GameGuard frames is characteristic of a global Windows hook (SetWindowsHookEx), which loads the DLL into processes across the desktop. That matches the observed behaviour: an idle Bun process, doing nothing but a timer tick, is killed the moment GameGuard appears.

0x3C is a NULL + struct-field-offset read — the injected code appears to expect something in the host process (a module handle, a TEB/PEB field, a hook target) and gets null back.

What I ruled out

Hypothesis | Ruled out by -- | -- Specific to a compiled/standalone executable | Reproduces on stock bun.exe Specific to the x64 (baseline) build | 1.3.14 repro is plain Windows x64 A regression in a specific Bun version | 1.3.14 and 1.4.0 crash identically Specific to one game | Reproduces on Helldivers 2 and Myth of Empires: Throne Startup / initialisation path | An already-running idle process is killed mid-flight Cumulative (memory pressure, GC, bad install, corrupt state) | Fully reversible; recovers on game exit with no reboot

Both games shipping the byte-for-byte identical 2025 build crash Bun. The game shipping an older, different build does not — despite starting GameGuard normally.

PE section layout differs between the two builds:

  • Crashing build (HD2 / MoE) — 9 sections, including .vlizer (~3 MB, executable) and .vm_sec. .vlizer is the section name used by Oreans Code Virtualizer; nearly the whole module is virtualized bytecode.
  • Non-crashing build (N-Age) — 3 sections (text/data/.rsrc), older plain packer, no virtualization engine.

Second crash signature (Bun 1.4.0, standalone executable)

This is the Bun runtime embedded in Anthropic's Claude Desktop (Claude Code 2.1.205), which is how I originally hit this. Same fault, same module:

Bun v1.4.0 (63bb0ca0d) Windows x64 (baseline)
Windows v10.26100
CPU: sse42 avx avx2
Features: Bun.stdin(2) jsc spawn standalone_executable claude_code
Elapsed: 233ms | RSS: 0.20 GB | Peak: 0.20 GB | Faults: 48935 | Machine: 33.72 GB
panic(thread 26712): Segmentation fault at address 0x3C
https://bun.report/1.4.0/e_163bb0camggggEkgggCCYnpggNT64.desy2TCYnpggNT64.desynouE_A2A4D

Frames: 0x2769 and 0x239079, both in npggNT64.des. Those offsets map onto the crashing build's sections as:

  • 0x2769 → .text (native hook stub, RVA 0x1000–0x30f72)
  • 0x239079 → .vlizer (virtualization engine, RVA 0x5d000–0x33ca00)

i.e. the injected hook enters at the native stub, calls into the virtualized engine, and faults there.

Open questions

  • Why Bun specifically? GameGuard injects broadly, but other processes on the same desktop survive. Something about Bun's process state seems to make GameGuard's hook read null at +0x3C. I have not been able to establish what, and I have deliberately not attached a debugger to the game or the anti-cheat.
  • Where a fix belongs is genuinely unclear. The faulting instruction is inside GameGuard's own virtualized code on its injected thread, so Bun cannot patch it directly. But if GameGuard is relying on an assumption about process state that Bun uniquely violates, a Bun-side mitigation may be possible. I'm raising it here because Bun is the only runtime I've seen this kill, and because the crash reporter attributes it to Bun.
  • GameGuard offers no exclusion list by design, and npggNT64.des is integrity-checked, so there is no safe user-side fix on the anti-cheat side.

Possibly related

#27940 reports the identical fault address (0x3C) with a Bun-based app. It may be the same cause if that reporter has GameGuard installed.

Environment

  • Windows 11, build 10.0.26100 (24H2)
  • CPU: sse42, avx, avx2 (no avx512)
  • RAM: 33.72 GB
  • nProtect GameGuard, npggNT64.des SHA-256 4227ed49…4f199, PE date 2025-04-21

title: Bun.build() leaks native memory per call (RSS unbounded, heapUsed flat) — kills long-lived watch/dev processes
author: tonioloewald
body:
### What version of Bun is running?

1.3.14 (darwin/arm64, Apple Silicon)

### What platform is your computer?

Darwin arm64 (macOS)

### What steps can reproduce the bug?

Every call to `Bun.build()` strands native memory that is never returned. RSS grows
without bound across sequential builds, while `heapUsed` stays completely flat — so it
is invisible to `Bun.gc(true)` and to any JS heap profiler.

Self-contained repro (no dependencies — it generates its own module graph):

```ts
// leak.ts — bun leak.ts [moduleCount] [iterations]
import { mkdirSync, writeFileSync, rmSync } from 'fs'
import { tmpdir } from 'os'
import { join } from 'path'

const MODULES = Number(process.argv[2] ?? 1500)
const ITERS = Number(process.argv[3] ?? 15)

const root = join(tmpdir(), `bun-build-leak-${process.pid}`)
const src = join(root, 'src')
mkdirSync(src, { recursive: true })

for (let i = 0; i < MODULES; i++) {
  writeFileSync(
    join(src, `m${i}.ts`),
    `export const v${i} = (x: number): number => {\n` +
      Array.from({ length: 20 }, (_, k) => `  x = (x + ${k}) * 1.0001`).join('\n') +
      `\n  return x + ${i}\n}\n`
  )
}
writeFileSync(
  join(src, 'entry.ts'),
  Array.from({ length: MODULES }, (_, i) => `import { v${i} } from './m${i}'`).join('\n') +
    `\nexport const all = [${Array.from({ length: MODULES }, (_, i) => `v${i}`).join(',')}]\n` +
    `console.log(all.length)\n`
)

const mb = (n: number) => (n / 1e6).toFixed(1).padStart(7)
let base = 0
for (let i = 1; i <= ITERS; i++) {
  const result = await Bun.build({
    entrypoints: [join(src, 'entry.ts')],
    outdir: join(root, 'out'),
    target: 'browser',
    ...(process.env.MINIFY ? { minify: true } : {}),
    ...(process.env.SOURCEMAP ? { sourcemap: 'linked' as const } : {}),
  })
  if (!result.success) { console.error(result.logs); break }
  Bun.gc(true) // force a full GC: anything still held is genuinely retained
  const { rss, heapUsed } = process.memoryUsage()
  if (i === 1) base = rss
  console.log(`build ${String(i).padStart(2)}  rss=${mb(rss)}MB  heapUsed=${mb(heapUsed)}MB  Δrss=${mb(rss - base)}MB`)
}
rmSync(root, { recursive: true, force: true })
```

```
bun leak.ts 1500 15                     # +51.6MB over 15 builds
MINIFY=1 SOURCEMAP=1 bun leak.ts 1500 15  # +83.7MB over 15 builds
```

### What is the expected behavior?

Memory used by a completed `Bun.build()` is released, so a process that bundles
repeatedly (a watch/dev server) has flat RSS across rebuilds.

### What do you see instead?

RSS grows monotonically per call and **does not plateau**. `heapUsed` stays flat, so
this is native, not JS-side.

Synthetic graph above (1500 modules, no plugins):

| config | 15 sequential builds |
|---|---|
| default | **+51.6 MB** |
| `minify` + `sourcemap: 'linked'` | **+83.7 MB** (~1.6× worse) |

A real app graph (a ~1.2 MB IIFE bundle: CodeMirror 6 + lezer + acorn), `minify` +
`sourcemap: 'linked'`, 40 sequential builds in one process:

```
build  1  rss=  116.8MB  heap=0.2MB  Δtotal=    0.0MB
build  5  rss=  215.8MB  heap=0.2MB  Δtotal=   99.0MB
build 10  rss=  269.1MB  heap=0.2MB  Δtotal=  152.3MB
build 20  rss=  340.5MB  heap=0.2MB  Δtotal=  223.8MB
build 30  rss=  437.7MB  heap=0.2MB  Δtotal=  320.9MB
build 40  rss=  483.7MB  heap=0.2MB  Δtotal=  367.0MB
```

**+367 MB over 40 builds (~9 MB per build), still climbing ~4–5 MB/build at build 40.**
`heapUsed` is 0.2 MB throughout, and every sample follows `Bun.gc(true)`.

### Why this matters (real-world impact)

This is not academic. Our static-site generator calls `Bun.build()` once per rebuild
inside a long-lived watch/dev server. Left running across ~2 days of editing (~2,500
rebuilds at ~55 MB stranded each), **the process reached ~136 GB RSS**, drove the
machine into swap thrash, and crashed it. Nothing in the JS heap showed anything wrong,
which made it very hard to find — the growth is entirely native.

Any long-lived process that bundles more than once hits this: dev servers, watch modes,
SSG/ISR rebuild loops, test harnesses that bundle fixtures.

### Workaround

Shelling out to the `bun build` **CLI** in a child process fixes it completely, because
the child's memory returns to the OS on exit. Same 15 real-graph bundles:

- in-process `Bun.build()`: **+192 MB**, still climbing
- subprocess `bun build`: **+0.5 MB**, flat after the first build

So the bundler's own work is fine; what leaks is whatever the in-process API retains
after `BundleV2` completes.

### Possibly related

- #30415 (native RSS grows linearly, heap flat)
- #31771 (`bun test --isolate`: linear RSS growth)

Same family — native RSS growing linearly while the JS heap stays flat — but a
different subsystem, so filing separately.

title: Bun.sql (Postgres): pool deadlocks at drain with mixed prepared read+write under pool oversubscription (concurrency >> max)
author: Beatloo-Labs

title: bun 1.4.0 is not faster than 1.3.14
author: l1t1
body:
```js
function fibonacci(n) {
  if(n < 1) return 0;
  if(n <= 2) return 1;
  return fibonacci(n - 1) + fibonacci(n - 2)
}

console.log(fibonacci(44))
```
```
//Bun v1.4.0-canary.1+9657f3789 (Windows x64)
C:\d>timer64 bun run fib.js
701408733


Kernel  Time =     0.031 =    1%
User    Time =     2.156 =   99%
Process Time =     2.187 =  101%    Virtual  Memory =    255 MB
Global  Time =     2.163 =  100%    Physical Memory =     48 MB

C:\d>timer64 bun1314 run fib.js
701408733


Kernel  Time =     0.062 =    2%
User    Time =     2.046 =   95%
Process Time =     2.109 =   98%    Virtual  Memory =    244 MB
Global  Time =     2.134 =  100%    Physical Memory =     54 MB
```
